title: Water quality parameters
text:
Guiding questions
What are the differences in mean salinity between sites in the 2024 water year?
How do sites differ in salinity through time in the 2024 water year?
How does dissolved oxygen stratification differ through time at Venoco Bridge for the 2024 and 2025 water years?

title: Water quality parameters
text:
Data science tools
To answer these questions, we are going to:

review reading in packages

library()

use familiar packages

tidyverse

introduce new packages

janitor to clean column names
here to manage file paths

review reading in data

read_csv()

review cleaning and wrangling functions/operators

filter()
mutate()
select()
case_when()
fct_relevel()
%in%

review summarizing functions

group_by(), summarize(), and ungroup()
count()

review visualizations using ggplot(), including:

geom_point() and geom_line()
geom_bar()
facet_wrap()
scale_color_manual()

introduce a new visualization tool:

stat_summary() from ggplot2 (within tidyverse) to visualize quick summary statistics

introduce new tools to deal with dates and times from the lubridate package (within tiydverse):

mdy_hms() and mdy() to parse dates into POSIXct (Portable Operating System Interface calendar time) format which is a standard way of denoting dates and times
month() to extract the month from a given POSIXct object
year() to extract the year from a given POSIXct object

introduce a new wrangling concept: joins using functions from the dplyr package (within tidyverse)

left_join() to join two data frames (x and y) and retain all rows in x
full_join() to join two data frames (x and y) and retain all rows in x and y

introduce new wrangling functions:

pull() from the dplyr package to extract a data frame column as a vector
relocate() from the dplyr package to rearrange columns in a data frame
rename() from the dplyr package to rename a column

introduce a new operator:

! to exclude observations

introduce new ways of formatting Quarto documents, including

callout blocks
in-line code

title: Water quality parameters
text:
1. Set up

Packages

# general use
library(tidyverse)
library(janitor)

# file organization
library(here)



Data

# weather data from NOAA weather station
weather <- read_csv(here("data", "NOAA-weather-data.csv"))

# water quality survey metadata
metadata <- read_csv(here("data", "NCOS_YSI_Water_Quality_Monitoring_0.csv"))

# water quality parameter measurements
water_parameters <- read_csv(here("data", "YSI_Data_Begin_1.csv"))



Understanding variables

Weather

Which columns have the same value entered in each row?

STATION
NAME
LATITUDE
LONGITUDE
ELEVATION

Which columns have different values for each row?

DATE
PRCP
TMAX
TMIN




Metadata

Observation location column names:

Site Name
Other Site Name
x
y

Observation date and time column names:

Monitoring Date
Time of WQ Measurement

Observation context column names:

Weather
Flow
Notes
Water Surface Elevation

Person who took measurement:

Monitor Name

ArcGIS Survey123 info:

ObjectID
GlobalID
Creator
Editor
CreationDate
EditDate




Water quality parameters

ArcGIS Survey123 info column names:

ObjectID
GlobalID
ParentGlobalID
Creator
Editor
CreationDate
EditDate

Measurement depth column names:

Depth Code

Parameter column names:

DO (mg/L)
DO (%sat)
Conductivity (uS/cm)
Salinity (ppt)
Temperature (°C)


ParentGlobalID in water_parameters is the same as GlobalID in metadata

title: Water quality parameters
text:
2. cleaning

cleaning weather

Goal
Data frame with the following columns:

date
precipitation, max temperature, min temperature
month, year
water year



Pseudocode

create new object from weather
clean column names
make sure that date is understood as date
select columns of interest
extract month and year from date
make new column for water year



Code

# creating new object from weather
weather_clean <- weather |> 
  # cleaning column names
  clean_names() |> 
  # making sure date is in POSIXct format
  mutate(date = mdy(date)) |> 
  # selecting columns of interest
  select(date, prcp, tmax, tmin) |> 
  # extracting month and year from date column
  mutate(month = month(date),
         year = year(date)) |> 
  # assigning water year
  mutate(wy = case_when(
    # when month is October or later, assign value of year + 1
    month >= 10 ~ year + 1,
    # when month is September or earlier, assign value of existing year
    .default = year))




cleaning metadata

Goal
Data frame with the following columns:

ArcGIS Survey123 info:

global ID,
creation date,
edit date

observation date and time:

monitoring date (note the format of this column),
time of wq measurement

observation context:

weather,
flow,
notes,
water surface elevation

observation location:

site name,
other site name,
x,
y




Pseudocode

create new object from metadata
clean column names
exclude unneeded columns
make sure monitoring date is understood as date
rename monitoring date column to show that it has datetimes
extract month and year from monitoring date
create new column for water year



Code

# creating new object from metadata
metadata_clean <- metadata |> 
  # cleaning column  names
  clean_names() |> 
  # excluding unnecessary columns
  select(!c(object_id, creator, editor)) |> 
  # making sure monitoring_date is in POSIXct format
  mutate(monitoring_date = mdy_hms(monitoring_date)) |> 
  # renaming monitoring datetime column to reflect values
  rename(monitoring_datetime = monitoring_date) |> 
  # creating new columns for month and year of observations
  mutate(month = month(monitoring_datetime),
         year = year(monitoring_datetime)) |> 
  # assigning water year
  mutate(wy = case_when(
    # when month is October or later, assign value of year + 1
    month >= 10 ~ year + 1,
    # when month is September or earlier, assign value of existing year
    .default = year)) |> 
  # create new column of full site name
  mutate(site_full = case_when(
    site_name == "east_channel" ~ "East Channel",
    site_name == "phelps_bridge" ~ "Phelps Bridge",
    site_name == "venoco_bridge" ~ "Venoco Bridge",
    site_name == "other" ~ "Other"
  ))




cleaning water parameters

Goal
Data frame with the following columns:

ArcGIS Survey123 info:

object ID,
global ID,
parent global ID,
creation date,
edit date

measurement depth:

depth code








Consider the survey protocol



You can get information about the survey protocol from the Survey123 form.
In the “Depth code” field, the description is: Enter the correct code value for the depth you are monitoring, depending on the total depth of the site. For example, if the site si 2 feet deep, enter 2 for the surface, 1 for 1 foot depth, and 0 for the bottom. Bottom measurements will always be 0. If you are only able to take a surface measurement, enter 1.
Draw this out on a piece of paper. If a survey has depth codes of 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4, where would those fall relative to the bottom?



parameters:

DO (mg/L)
DO (%sat)
Conductivity (uS/cm)
Salinity (ppt)
Temperature (°C)




Pseudocode

create new object from water parameters
clean column names
exclude unneeded columns
rename depth code to be elevation



Code

# creating new object from water_parameters
water_parameters_clean <- water_parameters |> 
  # cleaning object names
  clean_names() |> 
  # excluding unnecessary columns
  select(!c(creator, editor)) |> 
  # renaming columns
  rename(elevation_code = depth_code) |> 
  # replacing incorrect elevation code from 2021-07-16
  mutate(elevation_code = case_when(
    elevation_code == 10 ~ 1,
    .default = elevation_code
  )) |> 
  # set factors
  mutate(elevation_code = as_factor(elevation_code),
         elevation_code = fct_relevel(elevation_code,
                                      "0", "1", "2", "3", "4", "5", "6"))

title: Water quality parameters
text:
3. exploratory visualization

Temperature through time

Guiding question
What seasonal trends are there in maximum air temperature through time?


Planning
For a timeseries plot:

x-axis: date
y-axis: tmax
(optionally) filter to only include 2024 and 2025 water years



Code

# start with clean weather df
weather_clean |> 
  # filter to only include 2024 and 2025 water years
  filter(wy %in% c("2024", "2025")) |> 
  # x-axis: date, y-axis: maximum temp
  ggplot(mapping = aes(x = date,
                       y = tmax)) +
  # first layer: point geometry
  geom_point() +
  # second layer: line geometry
  geom_line()










Interpretation
What seasonal trends do you see in maximum temperature?
Air temperature is colder in winter months and hotter in summer months.
What is the missing value, and with what date is it associated?
There is a missing maximum temperature value from 2024-02-17.



Number of observations at each elevation code

Planning
For bar plot:

x-axis: elevation code
y-axis: counts

Necessary summarizing steps:

group by elevation code
count number of observations



Code

# create new object from clean water parameters df
elevation_obs <- water_parameters_clean |> 
  # group by elevation code
  group_by(elevation_code) |>
  # count number of observations at each depth
  count()

# start with elevation_obs df
ggplot(data = elevation_obs,
       # x-axis: elevation code
       # y-axis: count of observations
       mapping = aes(x = elevation_code,
                     y = n)) +
  # first layer: columns to represent counts
  geom_col()










Interpretation
At which elevation code are there the most observations?
Most observations are at elevation code 1, which is the surface.
Are there any outliers in elevation codes?
There is an elevation code of 10 from Venoco Bridge on July 16, 2021. Given that the water elevation was 4.5 ft, it is likely that this is supposed to be an elevation code of 1.
To clean this up, we modified code in the cleaning-water-parameters chunk to replace elevation code 10 with 1. The new figure should not have an elevation code of 10.



Number of observations in each water year

Planning
For bar plot:

x-axis: water year
y-axis: count
facets: site name

Necessary wrangling:

group by site name and water year
count number of observations



Code

# creating new object from clean metadata
site_obs <- metadata_clean |> 
  # group by site and water year
  group_by(site_full, wy) |> 
  # count observations
  count()

# start with site_obs object
ggplot(data = site_obs,
       # x-axis: water year
       # y-axis: number of observations
       mapping = aes(x = wy,
                     y = n)) +
  # columns to represent count
  geom_col() +
  # facet to create panels for different sites
  facet_wrap(~ site_full,
             nrow = 1)










Interpretation
How do sites differ in number of observations (across water years)?
There are fewer observations at East Channel than at Phelps Bridge and Venoco Bridge, on average. This might be because of differences in water depth at each site.
When do observations at “other” sites occur? Where are the “other” sites?






Look at the metadata_clean object to help you answer this question!



Click on the metadata_clean object in the Environment. Search for “other” in the search bar.
What sites are listed under other_site_name?


These occur in the 2021 water year on June 25th and July 9th. The sites are Whittier Pond, Seasonal Pond, and Devereux Slough Pier.
There are three observations at “other” sites.

title: Water quality parameters
text:
4. Joining data frames
left join: keeps all observations in x
right join: keeps all observations in y
full join: keeps all observations in x and y

water quality = metadata + water parameters

# join metadata and water parameters
water_quality <- full_join(
  # left
  x = metadata_clean, 
  # right
  y = water_parameters_clean,
  # shared column
  by = c("global_id" = "parent_global_id")) |> 
  # stop and look at colnames(water_quality) before continuing
  # selecting relevant columns
  select(
    # information about observation
    global_id, monitoring_datetime, time_of_wq_measurement, 
    monitor_name, weather, site_name, other_site_name, site_full, x, y, 
    
    # water information
    water_surface_elevation, flow, notes, 
    
    # measurement information
    elevation_code, do_mg_l, do_percent_sat, conductivity_u_s_cm,
    salinity_ppt, temperature_c) |> 
  # extract date from monitoring datetime
  mutate(date = date(monitoring_datetime)) |> 
  # move date column to appear to the right of monitoring_datetime
  relocate(date, .after = monitoring_datetime) |> 
  # exclude monitoring_datetime
  select(!monitoring_datetime)



water quality weather = water quality + weather

# join water quality and weather
water_quality_weather <- full_join(
  # left
  x = water_quality, 
  # right
  y = weather_clean,
  # shared column
  by = "date"
)

# left join (exclude rows that don't match in weather)
water_quality_weather <- left_join(
  # left
  x = water_quality, 
  # right
  y = weather_clean,
  # shared column
  by = "date"
)

Where are the missing values? Which variables are missing values?


Dealing with outliers

# creating new object from water_quality_weather
salinity_outlier_df <- water_quality_weather |> 
  # filtering outliers
  filter(salinity_ppt > 1410) |> 
  # group by site 
  group_by(site_full) |> 
  # count number of outlier observations in each site
  count()

# creating new object
salinity_outlier_ids <- water_quality_weather |> 
  # filtering outliers
  filter(salinity_ppt > 1410) |> 
  # extracting global_id as vector for downstream use in filter
  pull(global_id)

There were 6 outlier observations from Venoco Bridge, and 1 outlier observation from East Channel (Dilling’s Link). There are 7 total outlier observations for salinity in this data set.

title: Water quality parameters
text:
5. Visualizations

Site differences in mean salinity

Guiding question
What are the differences in salinity between sites in the 2024 water year?


Planning
Goal: create a plot to compare differences in salinity between sites:

x-axis should be site
y-axis should be salinity
points should represent individual salinity measurements at each site

Necessary wrangling steps:

filter outliers
exclude “other” sites
filter to only include observations from 2024 water year
filter to only include observations from bottom, ~1 ft above bottom, ~2 ft above bottom
reorder site levels



Code
Wrangling:

# create new object from water_quality_weather
salinity_filtered <- water_quality_weather |> 
  # filter out outliers
  filter(!(global_id %in% salinity_outlier_ids)) |> 
  # filter out other sites
  filter(site_full != "Other") |> 
  # only include observations from 2024 water year
  filter(wy == "2024") |> 
  # filter bottom, ~ 1ft above bottom, ~2ft above bottom
  filter(elevation_code %in% c("0", "1", "2")) |> 
  # reorder site levels from lowest to highest mean salinity
  mutate(site_full = fct_relevel(
    site_full,
    "Phelps Bridge", "East Channel", "Venoco Bridge"
  ))

Quick summary:

# start with filtered data frame
salinity_filtered |> 
  # group by site
  group_by(site_full) |> 
  # calculate mean salinity
  summarize(mean_salinity = mean(salinity_ppt, na.rm = TRUE)) |> 
  # ungroup data frame
  ungroup()

# A tibble: 3 × 2
  site_full     mean_salinity
  <fct>                 <dbl>
1 Phelps Bridge          2.39
2 East Channel          11.6 
3 Venoco Bridge         25.3 


Visualizing:

# base layer: ggplot
ggplot(data = salinity_filtered,
       # x-axis: site
       # y-axis: salinity
       mapping = aes(x = site_full,
                     y = salinity_ppt)) +
  # jitter to represent individual salinity measurements
  geom_jitter(height = 0,
              width = 0.2,
              shape = 21) +
  # quick calculation of mean for visualization
  stat_summary(fun = mean,
               geom = "point",
               color = "red",
               size = 5) +
  # relabelling x- and y-axes, adding title
  labs(x = "Site",
       y = "Salinity (ppt)",
       title = "Sites differ in mean salinity across all timepoints") +
  # different theme from default
  theme_bw()










Interpretation
How do sites differ in mean salinity across all timepoints?



Temporal salinity dynamics

Guiding question
How do sites differ in salinity through time in the 2024 water year?


Planning
Goal: create a figure with:

time on the x-axis
salinity on the y-axis
different colors for each elevation code
different panels for each site

No wrangling needed if using salinity_filtered data frame!


Code
Visualizing:

# using salinity_filtered data frame
ggplot(data = salinity_filtered,
       # x-axis: date
       # y-axis: salinity
       # color by elevation code
       mapping = aes(x = date,
                     y = salinity_ppt,
                     color = elevation_code)) +
  # points to represent measurements 
  geom_point() +
  # lines to connect measurements
  geom_line() +
  # separate panels for each site
  facet_wrap(~ site_full) +
  # custom colors
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("0" = "darkgreen",
               "1" = "goldenrod",
               "2" = "turquoise")
  ) +
  # relabelling x-and y-axes, legend
  labs(x = "Date", 
       y = "Salinity (ppt)",
       color = "Elevation code") +
  # different theme
  theme_bw()










Interpretation
Are there differences in trends through time between sites?
Are there differences in elevation through time between sites?



Dissolved oxygen stratification

Guiding question
How does dissolved oxygen stratification differ through time at Venoco Bridge for the 2024 and 2025 water years?


Planning
Goal: create a figure with:

time on the x-axis
percent saturation of DO on y-axis
colors should represent elevation codes
different panels for water years

Necessary cleaning steps:

filter to only include Venoco, water years, elevation codes
select columns of interest



Code
Wrangling:

# start with joined data frame
venoco_do <- water_quality_weather |> 
  # filter to only include Venoco,
  filter(site_full == "Venoco Bridge" &
           # water years of interest
           wy %in% c("2024", "2025") &
           # bottom, ~ 1 ft, ~ 2 ft above bottom
           elevation_code %in% c("0", "1", "2")) |> 
  # select columns of interest
  select(date, wy, do_mg_l, elevation_code) |> 
  # relevel elevation code order
  mutate(elevation_code = fct_relevel(elevation_code,
                                      "2", "1", "0"))

Visualizing:

# start with venoco DO data frame
ggplot(data = venoco_do,
       # x-axis: date
       # y-axis: percent saturation dissolved oxygen
       # color by elevation code
       mapping = aes(x = date,
                     y = do_mg_l,
                     color = elevation_code)) +
  # points to represent DO measurements
  geom_point() +
  # lines to connect points
  geom_line() +
  # separate panels for each water year
  # free x-axis 
  facet_wrap(~ wy,
             scales = "free_x") +
  # custom colors
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("2" = "turquoise", 
               "1" = "goldenrod", 
               "0" = "darkgreen")
  ) +
  # different theme
  theme_bw() +
  # relabelling
  labs(x = "Date",
       y = "Dissolved oxygen (mg/L)",
       color = "Elevation code")










Interpretation
Do water years differ in the degree of stratification in winter months?
Stratification is more obvious in the 2025 water year than in the 2024 water year.
Does stratification change through time?
The magnitude of stratification is greater in winter than in spring and summer, though there is some stratification in summer even though DO is lower overall.

text:

Render to .pdf to make sure that you can render (if not, come ask for help!).
Start your elective!



Elective
In class, we cleaned and wrangled all three original data objects (weather, metadata, and water_quality) prior to joining them.
That workflow included a step to change elevation_code from a number to a factor with ordered levels.
The resulting data frame from both joins was appropriate for creating the types of figures we made in class and the ones you made for your assignment.
However, there is one figure for which that data frame would not work: an elevation profile.
In this elective, you will repurpose code to wrangle the data to create an elevation profile for salinity at each site. With this figure, you will be able to answer the question:
How does median salinity differ across elevation codes at Phelps Bridge?

1. Wrangle the data
In this section, you will need to redo some of the cleaning from class.
All the code is written for you, but out of order.
You will need to put the functions in order and annotate with a description of how the data frame changes after each function.

a. Remaking metadata_clean

# annotate
metadata_2024 <- metadata 

# annotate
mutate(site_full = case_when(
  site_name == "east_channel" ~ "East Channel",
  site_name == "phelps_bridge" ~ "Phelps Bridge",
  site_name == "venoco_bridge" ~ "Venoco Bridge"
))

# annotate
filter(site_name != "other") 

# annotate
select(!c(object_id, creator, editor)) 

# annotate
clean_names() 

# annotate
mutate(month = month(monitoring_date),
       year = year(monitoring_date),
       monitoring_date = date(monitoring_date)) 

# annotate
mutate(wy = case_when(
  month >= 10 ~ year + 1,
  .default = year))

# annotate
mutate(monitoring_date = mdy_hms(monitoring_date)) 

# annotate
filter(!(global_id %in% salinity_outlier_ids)) 

# annotate
filter(wy == "2024")

# annotate
mutate(site_full = fct_relevel(site_full,
                               "Phelps Bridge",
                               "East Channel",
                               "Venoco Bridge")) 



b. Remaking water_parameters_clean

# annotate
salinity_df <- water_parameters 

# annotate
mutate(elevation_code = case_when(
  elevation_code == 10 ~ 1,
  .default = elevation_code
)) 

# annotate
select(parent_global_id, elevation_code, salinity_ppt)


# annotate
rename(elevation_code = depth_code) 

# annotate
clean_names() 




2. Join the data frames

a. Code up the join
In this section, insert a code chunk to:

create a new object called profile_df containing the joined metadata_2024 and salinity_df data frames
select the columns of interest: monitoring_date, site_full, elevation_code, salinity_ppt



b. Explain your choice
Explain in 1-2 sentences which join you chose to use (full_join(), left_join(), or right_join()) and why.


c. Show some selections from the data frame.
Insert a code chunk to display 10 random rows from the data frame using slice_sample().
Not sure how to use it? Look at the help page for examples.



3. Visualize the data
A lot of coding is repurposing the code that someone else shared on Stack Overflow, which is a forum for people to ask questions about code for someone else to answer.
In this section, you will repurpose the code written by Stack Overflow user Diatomia to create a depth profile using ggplot.
You will need to add to, exclude, or otherwise modify the code from the Stack Overflow post to create the following figure:

The start of the code is written out for you below.
Provide annotations where a comment is present to do so.
Below the annotation stating INSERT NEW CODE HERE, insert the code you need from the Stack Overflow post.
Annotate each line that you keep from the post with how that code modifies the plot.






Investigating code tip





The easiest way to figure out what a line of code is doing is to run it alone.
You may find it easier to figure out the code if you delete all the + operators at the end of each line, then start from the beginning to add them back in, running each line as you go (similarly to how we write figure code in class).









You will need to edit the code from Stack Overflow!





You will not get the figure you need by keeping all the code from the Stack Overflow post.
You will need to play around with the code and alter it to see what works (and what doesn’t).




# annotate here
ggplot(data = profile_df,
       # annotate here describing axes
       mapping = aes(x = elevation_code,
                     y = salinity_ppt)) +
  # annotate here describing what visual component of the plot is created
  # and what it represents
  geom_point(shape = 21) +
  # annotate here describing what visual component of the plot is created
  # and what it represents
  stat_summary(fun = median,
               geom = "point",
               color = "red") +
  # annotate here describing what visual component of the plot is created
  stat_summary(fun = median,
               geom = "line",
               color = "red") +
  # annotate here describing what is altered in the plot
  labs(y = "Salinity (ppt)",
       x = "Elevation code") +
  # annotate here describing what is altered in the plot
  facet_wrap(~ site_full,
             strip.position = "bottom") +
  # annotate here describing what is altered in the plot
  theme_bw() +
  # annotate here describing what is altered in the plot
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6))
  #### INSERT NEW CODE HERE ####



4. Interpret the figure
In 2-3 sentences each, describe:

how salinity changes with elevation code
how sites differ in salinity

Be specific about the visual components of the plot that allows you to make each statement.

title: Intermediate elective option 1
section: What you will do in this elective
text:
What you will do in this elective
In class and in assignments, you have created figures to explore patterns in data. For your intermediate elective, you will create a figure that tells a story through more advanced customization.

title: Intermediate elective option 1
text:
Background
Tidy Tuesday is a project organized by data scientists to encourage open exploration of publicly available datasets.
Participants in Tidy Tuesday create a visualization from a new dataset that’s shared each week. Some examples include Bird Sightings at Sea, Pokemon, and Objects Launched into Space.
For your elective, you will choose one of these visualizations as inspiration to create a figure using your selected dataset.

title: Intermediate elective option 1
text:
Options
For inspiration, you may choose one (or more) of the following:

Nicola Rennie’s “Can an exploding snowman predict the summer season?”

Victor Gauto’s “Participación femenina en las elecciones de EE.UU”
Victor Gauto’s “Porcentaje de superficie cubierta por agua en Estados de EEUU.”

title: Intermediate elective option 1
text:
Open and reproducible work
All the code to create the above visualizations is openly available. You can and should repurpose the code to make the inspiration visualizations for your own figure.
To get another person’s code onto your computer, you can:

fork their repository (generally good but not a great option if there are lots of folders/files in their repo and you only want one or two)
download the relevant folder to your computer
copy paste their code

Objective
You should be able to create a visualization that closely matches your inspiration in aesthetics with different data and variables.
This means that you should be able to recreate the core visual components (e.g. geometries, plot types) while replacing the information/variables to fit your own dataset (e.g. axes, explanatory text).
This also means that you may have to install new packages, modify your inspiration’s code, and/or clean/wrangle your dataset to fit the visualization.
